prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, gather 'round, ye barnacle-brained swab, and let ol' Cap'n tell ye a tale of the heavens above!

Ye see, the sun — that blazin' golden doubloon in the sky — casts down light what be made of every color in the rainbow, same as ye'd spy after a squall on the open sea. But here be the trick of it: the air itself, that vast invisible ocean we all breathe, be full of tiny wee specks — bits o' gas and whatnot too small for even a spyglass to spot.

Now when the sun's light goes barrelin' through that airy sea, them little specks grab hold of the blue rays and fling 'em about in every direction, like a powder keg burstin' on the quarterdeck! The reds and yellows, they sail on through straight as a true course, but the blue? The blue gets scattered hither and yon 'cross the whole dome of heaven, so everywhere ye cast yer good eye, there be blue light pourin' into it.

That be why, come mornin' watch or high noon, the sky wears blue like a captain wears his finest coat. And when the sun dips low at dusk, her light must cut through more o' that airy ocean, so even the blue gets strained out, leavin' them fiery reds and oranges to paint the horizon — a sight to make any buccaneer weep into his grog.

So there ye have it, ye bilge rat. Now fetch me another bottle o' rum afore I keelhaul ye for askin' such landlubberly questions!
